coredata.dc:description: A novel method is proposed for improving electromagnetic radiation of antenna in two distant bands independently and consists of nanocomposite based electric, magnetic materials coating on radiator suitable for flexible antenna design. Coplanar waveguide (CPW) fed flexible radiator is designed and fabricated on thin cotton substrate, the radiating patch consists of heptagon ring, rigid structure and printed on surface of substrate. CPW antenna consists of a signal strip placed in between two rectangular ground plane at one side of the substrate and suitable for flexible conformal designs. The rigid strip radiates upper band signal due to its smaller dimension and heptagon strip generate lower band due to its longer structure. A nanocomposite based electric material Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) is coated on rigid radiating patch that enhance radiation of upper band and nanocomposite based magnetic material nickel (Ni) nanoparticles on heptagon ring patch to improve lower band characteristics of antenna. GQDs offer high conductivity and low loss tangent through sp²-hybridized carbon atoms, quantum confinement, and edge effects, making them ideal for transparent, flexible antennas with superior signal transmission. Ni has unpaired 3d-electrons that produce magnetic moments due to their spin and orbital angular momentum, enabling high conductivity, permeability, and frequency-dependent properties ideal for antenna. Nanocomposite coated antenna has gain, bandwidth enhancement of 96 % and 90 % respectively as compared to antenna without nanocomposite materials. The fabricated antenna attained 2.4/5.2/5.8 GHz WLAN, ISM, 5 G sub 6 GHz, 2.4/5.0 GHz Wi-Fi and 2.5/3.5/5.5 GHz WiMAX bands, suitable for practical wireless applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel method is proposed for improving electromagnetic radiation of antenna in two distant bands independently and consists of nanocomposite based electric, magnetic materials coating on radiator suitable for flexible antenna design. Coplanar waveguide (CPW) fed flexible radiator is designed and fabricated on thin cotton substrate, the radiating patch consists of heptagon ring, rigid structure and printed on surface of substrate. CPW antenna consists of a signal strip placed in between two rectangular ground plane at one side of the substrate and suitable for flexible conformal designs. The rigid strip radiates upper band signal due to its smaller dimension and heptagon strip generate lower band due to its longer structure. A nanocomposite based electric material Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) is coated on rigid radiating patch that enhance radiation of upper band and nanocomposite based magnetic material nickel (Ni) nanoparticles on heptagon ring patch to improve lower band characteristics of antenna. GQDs offer high conductivity and low loss tangent through sp²-hybridized carbon atoms, quantum confinement, and edge effects, making them ideal for transparent, flexible antennas with superior signal transmission. Ni has unpaired 3d-electrons that produce magnetic moments due to their spin and orbital angular momentum, enabling high conductivity, permeability, and frequency-dependent properties ideal for antenna. Nanocomposite coated antenna has gain, bandwidth enhancement of 96 % and 90 % respectively as compared to antenna without nanocomposite materials. The fabricated antenna attained 2.4/5.2/5.8 GHz WLAN, ISM, 5 G sub 6 GHz, 2.4/5.0 GHz Wi-Fi and 2.5/3.5/5.5 GHz WiMAX bands, suitable for practical wireless applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Flexible antennas are essential in modern technology due to their adaptability, durability, and wide range of applications. Flexible antennas can be bent, folded, or stretched to fit non-flat surfaces, making them ideal for integration into various devices and materials, including wearables, vehicles, and structures. Flexible antennas are often made of resilient materials like polymers and conductive inks, which can withstand physical stress, bending, and environmental exposure. This durability is vital for applications where traditional rigid antennas would break or degrade. The thin and malleable nature of flexible antennas allows seamless integration into devices without adding bulk or compromising design. Flexible antennas can often be produced using cost-effective printing techniques such as screen printing or inkjet printing on flexible substrates. This reduces production costs while allowing scalability. The lightweight and compact form factor of flexible antennas ensures that they add minimal weight to devices, enhancing portability. Flexible antennas are pivotal in enabling next-generation technologies, including 5 G and beyond which support high-speed connectivity in complex environments, enable non-invasive monitoring with patch antennas on the skin. In energy harvesting, which use flexible solar panels or RF energy harvesting systems.

Cotton substrates are favoured in flexible radiator applications because they combine the necessary mechanical flexibility, favourable dielectric properties, comfort, and sustainability, making them well suited for integration into wearable and flexible electronic devices. NC materials are particularly suitable for flexible antenna applications because they combine high flexibility, enhanced mechanical strength tailored with electrical and dielectric properties, and lightweight form factors. These materials enable the development of advanced, high-performance antennas that can be integrated to different modern devices, from wearable electronics to flexible communication systems. The NC materials act as radiating elements by utilizing their unique combination of enhanced conductivity, dielectric and magnetic properties, and their ability to support surface plasmon resonance and controlled scattering. These properties enable NC based antennas to efficiently transmit and receive electromagnetic waves, making them highly suitable for various flexible, wearable, and high performance antenna applications.

A 40 mm× 30 mm wearable sensor antenna is proposed for 5 G application in [1]. Its design consists of characteristics mode analysis for performance which shows 3.8 dBi gain, 4.57–5.50 GHz bandwidth with good specific absorption rate. A flexible THz radiator is designed in [2] using polyimide substrate for 123 GHz and 180.4 GHz band operations. A less than −35 dB return loss (S11) is obtained at two resonance frequency and gain of lower, upper resonance frequencies are 4.12 dBi and 5.05 dBi respectively. A flexible MIMO antenna is designed in [3] consists of 4.63 dBi peak gain with 5.37–7.34 GHz bandwidth. The coupling effect in the radiator is minimized by introducing stub network. Flexible radiators are suitable for wearable, 5 G, Wi-Fi, wireless networks, 5 G SUB 6 GHz, ISM, and WBAN applications are detailed in [4-9]. The commonly used flexible substrates are cellulose laurate, nanoparticle based materials, polyethylene terephthalate, textile and synthetic paper are detailed in [10-15].

A circularly polarized antenna is detailed in [16] consist of ceramic material. It exhibit good mechanical properties using Al2O3 ceramic with its dielectric loss 0.003 and good dielectric constant. This polarized antenna consists of a dielectric resonator part and two hairpin filters for band pass characteristics. Its measured S11 is 48.00 % with peak gain 6.13 dBi shows good radiating characteristics in the operating band and is suitable for 5 G communication. A frequency reconfigurable liquid crystal radiator is mentioned in [17] consists of circular polarization. Radiating characteristics are increased by metasurface structure, temperature controlled liquid crystal and feed network. The relative dielectric constant of liquid crystal is varied by changing temperature and which generate frequency reconfigurability. This temperature controlled radiator change impedance bandwidth 39.0–40.3 % and gain by 11.59 dBi to 11.71 dBi.

A planar transmission strip basically called CPW, consists of a central strip and two ground planes separated by narrow gaps on the same substrate side. The CPW structure supports the propagation of electromagnetic waves while confining the fields between the strip and the ground planes. This makes it suitable for feeding antennas with minimal S11. The proposed NC coated radiator use CPW fed system, its dual band is obtained by the combination of heptagon ring and a rigid radiating element united to central CPW strip. The radiating strips being printed on cotton substrate surface (εr=1.6, tan(δ)= 0.04) with dimension 23.80 mm× 15.60 mm× 0.20 mm to form a flexible antenna. The flexible antenna radiation properties are improved by NC materials on the surface of heptagon ring and rigid strip. Since NC materials have excellent mechanical stability and good electrical characteristics. The dual band radiating patch consists of rigid and heptagon structure. The rigid strip radiate upper band signal due to its smaller dimension and heptagon strip generate lower band due to its longer structure. A NC based electric material GQDs is coated on rigid radiating patch that enhance radiation of upper band and NC based magnetic material Ni nanoparticles on the heptagon ring patch to improve lower band characteristics of antenna.

In [18] a multitask graphene electromagnetic detection device establishes a foundation for precise detection in applications utilizing second harmonic generation by the periodic arrangement of dielectric materials and the sensitivity of second harmonic wave peak shifts which enables multiscale detection of graphene thickness in a structure and haemoglobin concentration in blood types. A significant improvement in the concept and method of materials for electromagnetic radiations is demonstrated in the paper [19] and includes advancements in nanostructuring, compositional tuning, or advanced modelling to enhance electromagnetic properties such as permittivity, permeability, conductivity, or loss tangent by the use of nanomaterials like multitask janus metastructure.

The paper [20] introduces a novel antenna array design with slotted conductive slabs combining metasurface and defected ground plane techniques, tailored for flexible electronics and sensors in the millimeter-wave and terahertz spectrum. In [21] a compact 2 × 2 MIMO radiator for sub-6 GHz 5 G applications, featuring high isolation with enhanced gain through the integration of a frequency selective surface suitable for next-generation wireless communication systems is discussed. The paper [22] proposes a gradient-based optimization method for antenna arrays to achieve precise null control in specific regions and suppresses sidelobes. By using the Kreisselmeier-Steinhauser function, the maximum value of the peak sidelobe levels are described. A MIMO radiator with frequency-selective surface for 5 G millimeter-wave applications using Rogers RT/Duroid is presented in [23]. A four-element MIMO antenna design optimized for ISM, LTE, and 5 G sub-6 GHz applications, with a focus on achieving high isolation to minimize mutual coupling studied in [24]. The antenna demonstrates enhanced bandwidth, gain, and radiation efficiency, meeting the demands of high-speed, low-latency communication in modern wireless networks. The proposed antenna in [25] demonstrates excellent performance with its compact size, wideband operation, and significantly improved gain, making it a strong candidate for UWB systems requiring high gain by integrating a frequency-selective surface sheet. The transmitter utilizes direct antenna ASK modulation with a reconfigurable metasurface for point-to-point microwave links as discussed in [26]. The work in [27] introduces a compact 4-element MIMO antenna for sub-6 GHz with modified M-shaped strips and etched ground for high peak gain. The study in [28] introduces a cutting-edge THz on-chip antenna with 2 × 24 element array incorporating metasurface and artificial magnetic conductor methodology to meet elevated connectivity needs for wireless devices. A novel reconfigurable metasurface antenna in [29] using PIN photodiodes and an H-tree fractal slot design has controlled radiation characteristics with reduced size and enhanced bandwidth. Fabricated on FR4, the antenna performance, validated through measurements, makes it an efficient and compact solution for advanced RF and microwave applications. In [30] a compact virtual antenna array is proposed with a 25 dBi gain, 90 % efficiency, excellent isolation, and reduced energy consumption for sub-5 GHz wireless systems. The design and analysis presented in [31] discuss a high-gain 64-element microstrip antenna array for mm-wave 5 G communication systems to overcome path loss effect improving system coverage. The proposed array, based on a two-pronged fork microstrip patch radiator, achieves excellent performance.

The proposed flexible radiator with NC coated design detailed in Fig. 1, consist of seven design steps. The antenna 1 (AN1) is the basic CPW geometry, which consist of two CPW rectangular ground plane and a central narrow rectangular radiating strip. S11 of AN1 is detailed in Fig. 2.

S11 plot consists of 3.70 GHz resonance that occurs at −14.20 dB. This single band formation is due to central CPW strip with bandwidth of 3.1–4.41 GHz. In the dual band radiator central strip of radiator is designed for upper band. The upper resonance frequency of proposed radiator is 5.30 GHz. But AN1 resonance frequency is 3.70 GHz. An antenna 2 (AN2) is designed to obtain a high resonance frequency as compared to AN1, the central strip of AN1 is modified as showcased in Fig. 1. The radiating strip of AN2 is narrow and short as compared to AN1. AN2 resonance frequency is 5.00 GHz where −14.73 dB S11 is obtained as detailed in Fig. 2.

The antenna 3 (AN3) is designed for lower band and its radiating element consists of heptagon ring as mentioned in Fig. 1. AN3 resonance frequency is shifted to lower band since it has larger dimension as compared to narrow rigid element in AN2. The resonance frequency of AN3 is 3.45 GHz with −16.50 dB S11. The bandwidth of AN3 is 2.87–4.25 GHz as shown in Fig. 2.

AN2, AN3 radiating elements are combined to form dual band radiator antenna 4 (AN4) as summarised in Fig. 1. The rigid strip in AN4 dominate the high frequency band and heptagon ring for lower band. The simulated S11 of AN4 is detailed in Fig. 2 that illustrates 2.81–4.12 GHz bandwidth for lower band and 5.16–5.92 GHz for upper band, S11 at lower and upper resonance frequencies are −16.76 dB and −18.34 dB.

A nanocomposite based electric material (NEM) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) coated on the surface of heptagon ring and rigid radiating element of AN4, forms antenna 5 (AN5) as shown in Fig. 1. The S11 plot of AN5 illustrated in Fig. 3 shows that a good impedance bandwidth obtained in upper band. Since this NEM possess good radiation characteristics in high frequency band as compared to lower band. The dielectric characteristics of NEM supports high frequency signal than its low frequency components. Therefore the bandwidth of AN5 upper band is 4.83–6.27 GHz (1.44 GHz). But AN4 upper bandwidth is only 5.16–5.92 GHz (0.76 GHz). The bandwidth enhancement of AN5 upper band is 89 % as compared to AN4. The AN5 lower bandwidth is 2.53–3.88 GHz (1.35 GHz), but the AN4 lower band is with 2.81–4.12 GHz (1.31 GHz). This implies that bandwidth enhancement of AN5 lower band is only 3 % as compared to AN4 lower band.

The lower band radiation is enhanced by a second nanocomposite based magnetic material (NHM), nickel nanoparticles is introduced to coat AN4 radiating elements, resulting in antenna 6 (AN6) as shown in Fig. 1. The magnetic properties of the NHM accelerates the low frequency components. The simulated S11 of AN6 is illustrated in Fig. 3. The bandwidth of AN6 lower band is 2.37–3.97 GHz (1.60 GHz), the corresponding bandwidth of AN4 is only 2.81–4.1 2 GHz (1.31 GHz). The S11 value at lower resonance condition of AN6 is −23.87 dB, but AN4 corresponding value is only −16.76 dB. Therefore the S11 improvement in AN6 lower band is 42 % as compared to AN4. These simulated S11 and bandwidth of AN6 shows that NHM coating is apt for improving the radiation of lower band. But the NEM is suitable for upper band.

The summarized results of NC coating on the radiating elements shows that NEM coating on rigid element and NHM coating on the heptagon ring structure enhance the radiation of upper and lower band respectively. These optimized NC coating results are introduced in AN4 and to form antenna 7 (AN7). The proposed antenna (AN7) S11 being explained in Fig. 3. AN7 S11 of lower and upper band at resonance condition are −24.72 dB and −25.12 dB respectively. The corresponding S11 of AN4 is only −16.76 dB and −18.34 dB that concludes that AN7 produce good S11 at two resonance frequency. Similarly the bandwidth of lower and upper band of AN7 is 2.20–4.10 GHz (1.90 GHz) and 4.94–6.25 GHz (1.31 GHz) respectively. But the corresponding AN4 bandwidth are 1.31 GHz, 0.76 GHz respectively. The proposed radiator AN7 produce excellent radiation in both bands. The S11 variation of AN1- AN7 are summarized in Fig. 4.

Surface current distribution in antenna design refers to the pattern of electrical currents that flow along the surface of the antenna when is excited by a signal. This distribution is crucial in determining the antennas radiation characteristics, impedance, efficiency, and overall performance. Surface current distribution describes how currents are distributed over the conducting surfaces of an antenna. These currents are induced by the applied voltage or current at the feed point and are responsible for generating the electromagnetic fields that the antenna radiates.

The surface current distribution determines the antennas far-field radiation pattern, including the main lobe, side lobes, and nulls. Antennas are designed to operate efficiently at specific frequencies. The surface current distribution determines the resonant frequencies and influences the antennas electrical length, input impedance, and efficiency. Proper current distribution is essential for impedance matching and minimizing resistive losses to ensure maximum power transfer and optimal radiator performance.

The proposed CPW radiator consists of two radiating elements, heptagon and a shorter rigid strip. The surface current plot of AN4 at two resonance conditions are illustrated in Fig. 5. At lower resonance frequency (3.40 GHz) the heptagon ring structure dominates major surface current distribution as compared to rigid radiating element. The left and right side portion of the heptagon has more current flow as compared to the central rigid strip as detailed in Fig. 5. Since heptagon ring structure support high wavelength signal due to its larger dimension as compared to rigid element. At high resonance frequency (5.30 GHz) current distribution is along rigid element due to its smaller dimension. Since shorter strip radiate low wavelength signal (high frequency signal) and more current flow along the shorter rigid strip as compared to heptagon structure detailed in Fig. 5.

The Fig. 6 concludes the current distribution of AN7 at two resonance frequencies. The maximum value of magnitude of surface current at lower resonance frequency is 170.0862 A/m and upper resonance frequency is 175.5623 A/m as detailed in Fig. 6. But the corresponding current distribution of AN4 is 140.3310 A/m and 156.2685 A/m as mentioned in Fig. 5. The magnitude of AN7 current distribution at lower and upper resonance frequencies are higher than the corresponding surface current distribution of AN4 illustrated in Fig. 5. This distribution comparison implies is that NC material coated antenna (AN7) has more current distribution as compared to copper strip antenna (AN4). This higher current distribution improves electrical characteristics of antenna like gain, bandwidth and S11 values.

The distribution of electric and magnetic fields determine antenna performance because they directly influence transmitting and receiving electromagnetic waves. Electric (E) and magnetic (H) field distributions are used to derive the radiation pattern of radiator which calculates the radiated or received signal in different directions. This helps in designing antennas with desired directivity and gain for specific applications, such as omnidirectional for broadcasting or directional for point-to-point communication. Electric and magnetic field distribution plots provide essential insights into how an antenna interacts with its environment, radiates energy, and performs under operating conditions.

The E and H field distribution of AN7 at two resonance frequencies are illustrated in Fig. 7, which shows that at 5.30 GHz a high electric field distribution obtained on the rigid strip and magnetic field along the heptagon ring at 3.40 GHz. This shows that NEM on the rigid strip improve electric field distributions and NHM is on the heptagon structure increase magnetic field distribution. These field distribution illustrate that electric and magnetic field are distributed on two different radiating strips, to generate two independent frequency bands.

The proposed antenna radiating patch, substrates are optimised using HFSS tool. The detailed antenna measurements are A1 = 23.80 mm, A2 = 5.80 mm, A3 = 4.75 mm, A4 = 0.80 mm, A5 = 3.00 mm, A6 = 5.50 mm, A7 = 5.85 mm, A8 = 5.05 mm, A9 = 0.95 mm, and A10 = 7.55 mm as mentioned in Fig. 8. This proposed antenna is fabricated using cotton material for flexible radiating characteristics as shown in Fig. 8.

Connecting a SubMiniature version A (SMA) connector to a CPW antenna involves ensuring a seamless transition of the RF signal from the coaxial structure of the SMA connector to the planar structure of the CPW. This requires careful mechanical and electrical integration to minimize signal reflections, impedance mismatches, and losses. An SMA connector is a coaxial connector with inner conductor carring the RF signal. Outer conductor serves as the ground. Dielectric insulator separates the inner and outer conductors. The CPW signal trace and ground plane aperture are optimized to maintain a 50 Ω characteristic impedance, ensuring seamless integration with the SMA connector for efficient signal transmission. The centre pin of the SMA is soldered to the CPW signal trace, ensuring a reliable electrical connection. The SMA connector outer shell is united to the CPW ground planes as shown in Fig. 8.

GQDs, with high electrical conductivity (10² to 10⁴ S/m) and low loss tangent (0.01–0.1), exhibit unique electrical and dielectric properties due to quantum confinement and edge effects, making them ideal for transparent, flexible antennas with excellent signal transmission. Synthesized via bottom-up methods by carbonizing organic precursors like citric acid or glucose at 180–200°C, GQDs are purified to produce high-performance materials for advanced electronic applications. The electromagnetic simulation software, Ansys High-Frequency Structure Simulator (HFSS) are used for simulation of antenna design and optimization. In ANSYS HFSS materials library the new material GQDs are added with electromagnetic parameters like relative permittivity εr= 4.55, loss tangents tan (δ) = 0.01 and conductivity σ = 10⁴ S/m and assign the GQDs to the rigid strip of the antenna during simulation.

In ANSYS HFSS materials library the new material Ni are added with electromagnetic parameters like relative permeability μr= 325, conductivity σ = 1.43 × 107 S/m and skin depth of 0.66 µm at 4 GHz and assign Ni to the heptagon strip of the antenna during simulation.

The current distribution on a CPW antenna is governed by the electromagnetic fields in the CPW structure and depends on its geometry, operating frequency, and boundary conditions. A general mathematical representation can be understood by analysing the behaviour of the CPW structure. For a CPW-fed antenna, the current distribution

Antenna parameters S11, gain, radiation efficiency and polarization plot are measured by using reference antenna, anechoic chamber and vector network analyzer. Reference antenna is a well-characterized antenna with known polarization. Anechoic chamber reduces reflections for accurate measurement. Vector network analyzer or spectrum analyzer is to measure transmitted or received signals. Antenna measurement system includes a signal generator, receiver, and rotating platform being placed in the anechoic chamber for removing the external disturbance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radiation behaviour of nanocomposite based electric and magnetic materials
ce:section.ce:para:
The GQDs are nanometre-sized particles of graphene that exhibit quantum properties [32]. These materials have significant class of carbon-based nanomaterials due to their unique electrical, optical, and chemical properties. GQDs typically range in size from 2 to 20 nm and are characterized by their high surface area, excellent conductivity, and quantum confinement effects, which make them ideal for various applications, including conductive fillers in composite materials. Quantum confinement occurs when the particle size is comparable to the exciton Bohr radius, leading to discrete electronic states. This results in unique electrical and optical properties that differ from bulk graphene or larger graphene sheets. The quantum confinement effect leads to the appearance of an energy bandgap in GQDs, making them semiconducting or insulating, depending on the size.
(10)

GQDs retain the excellent electrical conductivity of graphene. The presence of sp²-hybridized carbon atoms allows for efficient charge carrier mobility, contributing to their role as conductive fillers in composite materials. The NC with conductive fillers GQDs exhibit unique electrical and dielectric properties because of their quantum confinement, edge effects and is taken as NEM in antenna. These composites are with high electrical conductivity in the range of 102 to 104 S/m attributed to the delocalized π-electrons in their graphene structure. GQDs typically have a low loss tangent, around 0.01–0.1 as they dissipate minimal energy. A lower loss tangent means that GQDs can efficiently store and transfer energy, making them favourable in high-performance electronic applications and suitable for transparent and flexible antenna designs, excellent signal transmission. Preparation of GQDs composites by using the bottom-up methods. These involve building up GQDs from molecular carbon sources. The first step involves carbonization of organic precursors like citric acid, glucose, or polyethylene glycol. The organic precursor is heated (typically in the range of 180–200°C), causing it to dehydrate and carbonize into small carbon nanoparticles. As the carbonization progresses, small carbon quantum dots are formed. The synthesized GQDs are purified via centrifugation or filtration [33], [34].

GQDs, with high electrical conductivity (10² to 10⁴ S/m) and low loss tangent (0.01–0.1), exhibit unique electrical and dielectric properties due to quantum confinement and edge effects, making them ideal for transparent, flexible antennas with excellent signal transmission. Synthesized via bottom-up methods by carbonizing organic precursors like citric acid or glucose at 180–200°C, GQDs are purified to produce high-performance materials for advanced electronic applications. HFSS is used for simulation of antenna design and optimization. In ANSYS HFSS materials library the new material GQDs are added with electromagnetic parameters like relative permittivity εr= 4.55, loss tangents tan (δ) = 0.01 and conductivity σ = 10⁴ S/m and assign the GQDs to the rigid strip of the antenna during simulation.

Ni nanoparticles improve the magnetic permeability of antennas. This helps in better transmission and reception of electromagnetic waves. Ni nanoparticles are widely used in applications such as magnetic materials, catalysts, batteries, and sensors. Ni nanoparticles are synthesised by the chemical reduction method. In this method, Ni salt (nickel chloride NiCl2) reduced in solution using reducing agents such as sodium borohydride (NaBH₄). A solution of Ni salt in water is prepared with concentration of 0.1 M to 0.5 M. NaBH₄ reducing agent stirring at controlled temperature of 20°C to 50°C. Reducing agent concentration to nickel salt is 2:1 to ensure complete reduction of Ni ions. Moderate stirring at 300–800 rpm for 45 minutes is typically sufficient to ensure uniform mixing and to prevent particle agglomeration. The Ni nanoparticles precipitate out of the solution [35]. The particles are collected, washed, and dried. The advantage of this method being simple and cost-effective. The overall reaction is summarized by,

The Ni has a relative magnetic permeability of approximately 100–600 μ. The high permeability helps in improving antenna performance by enhancing magnetic field interactions. The electrical resistivity of Ni is around 6.99 μΩ cm at room temperature and it is suitable for efficient current flow in antennas. The thermal conductivity of Ni is approximately 90 W/m K reducing the risk of overheating in high-power antennas. Ni provides excellent electromagnetic interference shielding, especially at 1 MHz to 10 GHz, with typical effectiveness of 60–90 dB. This reduces interference from external electromagnetic noise, improving antenna signal clarity [36].

In ANSYS HFSS materials library the new material Ni are added with electromagnetic parameters like relative permeability μr= 325, conductivity σ = 1.43 × 107 S/m and skin depth of 0.66 µm at 1 GHz and assign Ni to the heptagon strip of the antenna during simulation.

S11 being a crucial parameter in antenna design that quantifies the amount of reflected power from the radiator due to impedance mismatch between the radiator and feeding system. Antennas are studied to operate at specific frequencies with a characteristic impedance, typically 50 ohms. A low return loss (high negative dB value) indicates good impedance matching, meaning most of the power from the transmitter is delivered to the antenna for radiation. S11 is used to define the operational bandwidth of radiator. The frequency range over which S11 is below a specific threshold (-10 dB) is considered the antennas usable bandwidth.

Antenna gain is a fundamental parameter representing the ability of radiator to direct or concentrate EM energy in a particular direction relative to an isotropic radiator and is measured in decibels. Gain measures how well an antenna converts input power into useful power in a specific direction. High gain implies stronger signals in the intended direction, improving the communication range and coverage area.

Polarization plots are essential in antenna design as they provide a graphical representation of the antennas polarization characteristics, describing how the EM waves radiated or received by the radiator oriented in space which offers valuable insights into radiator performance and compatibility with communication systems. Polarization ensures the antenna is compatible with the polarization of the transmitter or receiver to maximize signal reception and minimize losses. Mismatched polarizations lead to polarization loss, reducing signal strength. Polarization plots guide designers in aligning antennas for maximum energy transfer and system efficiency. Cross-polarization indicates the level of unwanted polarization components. Polarization plots are used to measure and minimize cross-polarization levels, ensuring cleaner signals and reduced interference.

The AN4, AN5 measured and simulated gain are detailed in Fig. 9 for the lower band 3.30–3.80 GHz and upper band 4.80–6.40 GHz. Simulated and observed gain of AN4 and AN5 are matched in both bands. AN5 upper band gain is higher than AN4 upper band gain. But lower band gain of AN4 and AN5 are only a small variations. The average value of measured gain of AN5 upper band is 2.30 dBi, but AN4 average value is only 1.49 dBi. The corresponding values of lower band are 1.88 dBi and 1.45 dBi. Average observed gain variation of upper band is 0.81 dBi, but in lower band it is 0.43 dBi. This gain analysis shows that AN5 is suitable for improving the performance of upper band due to the NEM.

Simulated and measured gain of AN4, AN6 are illustrated in Fig. 10 and which implies that both bands are correlated. AN6 observed gain of lower band is superior to AN4 lower band gain. The average measured gain of AN6 lower band is 2.65 dBi but the corresponding value of AN4 is only 1.45 dBi. The lower band average gain of AN6 is improved by 1.20 dBi as compared to AN4. These simulated and measured results of AN4, AN6 shows that AN6 lower band radiation is higher than AN4 lower band. The NHM coating on AN6 radiator is suitable for performance enhancement in lower band as compared to upper band.

The gain plot of AN4, AN5 and AN6 results shows that NEM coating of AN5 radiator is suitable for performance enhancement in the upper band and NHM coating on the AN6 is apt for enhancing the performance of lower band. This summarized results are introduced for the fabrication of AN7, which means NEM coating on the rigid patch improve the performance of upper band and NHM coating on the heptagon ring structure enhance radiation of lower band. The simulated, measured gain of AN4 and AN7 are detailed in Fig. 11.

The average measured value of lower and upper band of AN4 is 1.45 dBi and 1.49 dBi respectively. The corresponding value of AN7 is 2.95 dBi and 2.80 dBi. Therefore the gain is enhanced in AN7 by 103 % at lower band and upper band with 88 % compared to AN4. Also observed peak gain of AN7 in lower, upper band are 3.20 dBi and 3.05 dBi respectively. But corresponding value of AN4 is only 1.60 dBi and 2.00 dBi. This measured and simulated gain plot implies that AN7 is suitable for both lower and upper band radiation. The AN4-AN7 gain are summarized in Fig. 12.

The simulated and observed S11 of AN4, AN5 are detailed in Fig. 13. Its simulated and observed S11 are correlated in both bands. The measured upper bandwidth of AN5 is 4.77–6.35 GHz (1.58 GHz), but the corresponding AN4 is 5.15–5.81 GHz (0.66 GHz). Therefore the upper bandwidth is enhanced by 139 % in AN5 as compared to AN4. But the measured lower bandwidth of AN5 is 2.52–3.92 GHz (1.40 GHz) and AN4 is 2.81–4.14 GHz (1.30 GHz). The lower bandwidth improved in AN5 is only 7.70 % as compared to AN4. Moreover the AN5 obtained −23.80 dB minimum S11 at 5.41 GHz resonance condition. But in AN4 it is only −17.75 dB at 5.47 GHz. This measured results shows that AN5 is suitable for upper band as compared to AN4.

The AN6 is fabricated and the S11 is illustrated in Fig. 14. The measured S11 shows that −24.92 dB at 3.12 GHz, the corresponding S11 of AN4 is only −16.90 dB and proves that AN6 is suitable for lower band.

The summarized results of AN5 and AN6 shows that it is suitable for upper and lower band respectively. The AN7 is obtained by combination of results of AN5 and AN6. That is heptagon ring structure coated with NHM and the rigid by NEM. The fabricated AN7 S11 is detailed in Fig. 15 which consist of improved S11 and bandwidth. The lower and upper bandwidth of AN7 are 2.10–4.15 GHz (2.05 GHz) and 4.70–6.40 GHz (1.70 GHz). When compared to AN4, the AN7 bandwidth enhancement is 54 % (lower band) and 157 % (upper band). The minimum S11 of lower and upper band of AN7 are −25.25 dB and −25.84 dB, the corresponding AN4 is only −16.90 dB and −17.75 dB. The measured S11 of AN4-AN7 are summarized in Fig. 16.

The polarization plot of AN7 is showcased in Fig. 17. The simulated and measured polarization plot are in good agreement in both resonance frequencies. The co-pol magnitude is higher as compared to cross-pol at 3.40 GHz and 5.30 GHz. The impedance parameter of AN7 is discussed in Fig. 18, which consists of real part of Z11 that tends to 50 Ω matching condition at 3.40 GHz and 5.30 GHz resonance frequency.

The magnitude of VSWR plot of AN4-AN7 are compared in Fig. 19. The VSWR values tends to unity at 3.40 GHz and 5.30 GHz resonance frequency, which shows that radiators are in good matching at two operating bands.

The flexibility characteristics of radiator is evaluated at different bending condition. Convex E and H-plane bending analysis of AN7 calculated by three bending radius R= 10 mm, 20 mm, and 30 mm. The Fig. 20 and Fig. 21 details E, H-plane bending condition for S11 measurements. It also illustrates the S11 comparison of rigid and flexible antenna with simulated and measured values. The flexible antenna covers all frequency components in the rigid antenna. These bending analysis shows that AN7 is suitable for flexible radiator applications.

The radiation efficiency of AN7 is evaluated and is displayed in Fig. 22. The average value is 91 % (lower band) and 93 % (upper band). The lower and upper band simulator, observed results are matched.

This flexible radiator AN7 is also suitable for wearable applications. Its wearable application is evaluated on the surface of cotton cloth and on the body surface. The S11 plot of wearable condition is evaluated and compared with rigid radiator characteristics, which conclude that it is suitable for wearable applications. The S11 plot on the surface of cotton cloth and on the body surface are mentioned in Fig. 23 and Fig. 24.

Different NC based antenna comparisons are discussed in Table 1. Zinc aluminate and titanium oxide are used for the microstrip patch antenna applications. This fabricated antenna operate with enhanced bandwidth and VSWR is less than 2. The fabricated antenna produce gain 2.14 dBi, diversity 3.58 dBi and suitable for X band applications detailed in [44]. Fumed silica are used in RT-duroid material to obtain a good performance enhancement. This fabricated antenna possess 10 dBi gain, radiation efficiency 96 % and suitable for C and X band applications mentioned in [45]. A compact patch antenna design is realized by barium ferrite and epoxy NC materials. The miniaturization factor of radiator is obtained by 2.05 in the frequency range 1.3 GHz is detailed in [46]. A microwave antenna is introduced by low dielectric constant and minimal loss material based on polyimide NC is discussed in [47]. This proposed NC produce signal strength 40 % more compared to FR4 material. A High performance flexible antenna based on polymer NC film is explained in [48], [49]. This suggested radiator generate −37.50 dB S11 at resonance condition with 76.7 % radiation efficiency and is suitable for bluetooth and Wi-Fi communications. The substrate consists of sillenite-cobaltite NC are used for fabrication of X band communication is mentioned in [50]. This fabricated antenna results are coherent with designed values and only 0.39 % change in the resonance frequency. The compact radiator design for wireless operation in [51], [52] can also be adapted to real time biomedical wireless applications implemented in [53], [54].

Measured bandwidth of proposed radiator is 2.05 GHz (lower band), 1.7 GHz (upper band) with average gain, radiation efficiency are 2.88 dBi and 91 % respectively. The bandwidth of ceramic nanomaterial based rigid radiator mentioned in [43] produce low bandwidth (0.22 GHz) and radiation efficiency compared to proposed radiator. The nonflexible radiator detailed in [39] consists of bismuth-cobalt-based material and which has low radiation efficiency (75 % - 85 %) as compared with proposed antenna. Carbon-coated cobalt and polyaniline based rigid FR4 radiator mentioned in [37] produce bandwidth 0.43 GHz and 0.41 GHz, which very small as compared proposed antenna bandwidth (2.05 GHz and 1.7 GHz). Therefor the proposed antenna has superior radiation with good bending characteristics.

This proposed radiating strip printed on cotton substrate surface (εr=1.6, tan(δ)= 0.04) with thickness 0.20 mm to form a flexible antenna. This CPW antenna design consists of dielectric inserted loss due to the loss of signal power generated in presence of a dielectric material within or near the waveguide structure. This loss occurs as a result of the interaction between the electromagnetic fields and the dielectric material. Dielectric loss is related to the materials loss tangent tan(δ), which measures how much energy is dissipated as heat relative to the energy stored in the dielectric. The tan(δ) of proposed NC based antenna is 0.04, which is small and which possess less dielectric loss. Thicker dielectric layers can significantly increase dielectric loss, but the proposed flexible cotton substrate has low thickness (0.20 mm) and produce low dielectric loss.

Electromagnetic property of antenna is enhanced by the addition of NC based electric and NC based magnetic materials. This proposed NC based materials are coated on two radiating patch of antenna and improves the radiation of each band independently. These proposed NC materials produce good mechanical stability and electrical characteristics, which is apt for flexible radiator applications. Flexibility behaviour of radiator analysed at different scenario and results are correlated to wearable applications. Radiation parameter measurements shows that a high degree of enhancement as compared to radiator with traditional patch antenna. The proposed NC coated radiator is suitable for practical wearable, flexible wireless applications. GQDs offer high conductivity and low loss tangent through sp²-hybridized carbon atoms, quantum confinement, and edge effects, making them ideal for transparent, flexible antennas with superior signal transmission. Ni has unpaired 3d-electrons that produce magnetic moments due to their spin and orbital angular momentum, enabling high conductivity, permeability, and frequency-dependent properties ideal for antenna. The nanocomposite coated antenna has gain, bandwidth enhancement of 96 % and 90 % respectively as compared to antenna without nanocomposite materials. The fabricated radiator includes 2.40/5.20/5.80 GHz WLAN, ISM, 5 G sub 6 GHz, 2.40/5.00 GHz Wi-Fi and 2.50/3.50/5.50 GHz WiMAX bands, suitable for practical wireless antenna applications.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para:
Image 1
Abhilash S. Vasu is currently pursuing his Ph.D. in Electronics and Communication Engineering from Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education, Kumaracoil, Kanyakumari, Tamil Nadu, India. He has obtained B.Tech in Electronics and Communication Engineering from Government Engineering College, Trivandrum, Kerala, India and M.Tech in Electronics and Communication Engineering from College of Engineering, Trivandrum, Kerala, India with university second rank. He has several International Journal and conference publications. His current research interest includes Antenna Design and Microwave communication.

Image 2
Dr. T.K. Sreeja, Associate Professor, Department of Nanotechnology, Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education, has completed her Ph.D from the Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education and has completed her M. E (Communication Systems) from the Mepco Schlenk Engineering College, Sivakasi, Tamilnadu. She has teaching experience for 15 years and research experience for 10 years. Her specialised areas in research are Flexible and Wearable Multiband Antenna Design for various applications. She has published 2 Books/ Book Chapters. She has the credit of having published many research papers in International Journal publications. She is now guiding 5 Ph.D scholars.

Image 3
Lakshmi N R is currently pursuing his Ph.D. in Electronics and Communication Engineering from Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education, Kanyakumari, Tamilnadu, India. She has obtained B.Tech in Electronics and Communication Engineering from Amal Jyothi College of Engineering, Kanjirapally, Kottayam, Kerala, India and M.Tech in Information and Communication Engineering from Regional Center of Anna University, Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India with university first rank. She has many International Journal publications. Her current research interest includes Antenna Design, Slot Antenna.

Image 4
Silpa Ajith Kumar is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Electronics and Instrumentation Engineering, Dayananda Sagar College of Engineering, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India. She holds an M.E.,degree in Applied Electronics. Her research areas are machine learning, deep learning, RF design and automation in process control. She is pursuing a Ph.D. in the area of biomedical image processing. She has authored or co-authored over 12 internationally refereed articles her research interests include computer vision, biomedical instrumentation, image processing, IoT, medical image analysis and RF design.
